planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in any well-drained to moist soil in any light condition from full sun to deep shade; this is one of the most adaptable native plants for the South.

directions: Plant in any soil — wet or dry, acidic or alkaline, rich or poor, sun or deep shade — wax myrtle tolerates virtually every condition in zone 7–11 once established and is arguably the Gulf Coast's most versatile and valuable native shrub. The aromatic, evergreen foliage provides year-round screening and privacy; both male and female plants produce tiny waxy blue-gray berries that encrust the branches in fall and winter, providing essential food for yellow-rumped warblers, tree swallows, and many other bird species that can digest the high-fat wax. The root nodules fix atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil over time. Prune heavily at any time to maintain a desired size, or allow it to develop naturally into a multi-trunked small tree 15–20 feet tall. The aromatic leaves have traditional use as an insect repellent.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in virtually any soil; yaupon is one of the most adaptable native shrubs available. Female plants produce abundant red berries — plant at least one male per five females for berries, or choose a self-fertile cultivar.

directions: Plant in any soil — sandy, clay, wet, or extremely dry — in full sun to deep shade; yaupon is the only native North American plant containing caffeine and has a long history of preparation as a beverage tea in the Southeast before European colonization. Lightly roasted leaves brewed like green tea produce a pleasant, mild caffeinated drink without the bitterness of coffee. As a landscape plant it is among the most adaptable and maintenance-free native shrubs for zone 7–10 — surviving salt spray, drought, flooding, clay, shade, and deer browsing that defeat most plants. The dense branches provide excellent nesting habitat; small red berries on female plants feed mockingbirds, robins, and cedar waxwings through winter. Available in upright, weeping ('Pendula'), and compact dwarf cultivars for varied landscape uses.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in partial shade in moist, well-drained soil; in zone 9–11 it acts as a perennial or self-sowing annual, persisting year-round in frost-free areas.

directions: Direct sow in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade after temperatures warm; surinam spinach (also called Philippine spinach or waterleaf) is a heat-loving tropical leafy vegetable used throughout West Africa, the Caribbean, and Southeast Asia, where the succulent leaves and stems are harvested young and cooked in soups, stews, and stir-fries. In zone 9–11 it is remarkably productive in the hot, partially shaded spots where most greens fail completely, filling a critical gap for summer leafy vegetable production under trees or on north-facing beds. It is tender to frost but self-sows prolifically in warm climates to return each year. Harvest the tender growing tips regularly to encourage continuous bushy production. The small magenta flowers are ornamental and attract bees.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in acidic, well-drained, sandy to loamy soil; avoid clay. Do not over-fertilize — sparkleberry grows naturally in very poor, acidic, sandy soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), poor to average sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; sparkleberry is the largest native blueberry relative in the Southeast, growing to 25 feet as a gnarly, multi-trunked small tree with distinctive exfoliating gray-brown bark that reveals warm cinnamon-red underbark — one of the most ornamental native bark patterns in southern woodland plants. The white bell-shaped flowers in spring are followed by small, persistent dark black berries that, while dry and astringent compared to commercial blueberries, are eagerly consumed by wood thrushes, mockingbirds, foxes, and other wildlife throughout fall and winter. It grows naturally in dry, sandy upland forests and longleaf pine savannas. An outstanding specimen tree for dry, sunny or partly shaded naturalistic landscapes.

planting.note: Plant tubers or potted divisions in shallow water (1–6 inches deep) or at the margin of a pond or rain garden in full sun to partial shade once water temperature reaches 60°F.

directions: Plant tubers or divisions in shallow water (1–6 inches deep) or in saturated soil at a pond or stream margin in full to partial sun; arrowhead is a native aquatic perennial with distinctive arrow-shaped leaves, three-petaled white flowers in midsummer, and underwater starchy tubers (called wapato or katniss) that were a critically important food source for many Native American peoples across North America, harvested by wading and loosening the tubers with the feet in fall when starches are concentrated. The tubers are nutritious and delicious when boiled or roasted like small potatoes. The plant also provides outstanding wetland habitat — frogs shelter in the leaves, diving ducks (especially mallards and teals) eat the tubers, and shorebirds and herons hunt at its margins. In favorable conditions it spreads aggressively; contain in submerged containers if space is limited.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops
